metadata.title: Supplemental Material for Prevalence, Correlates, and Drivers of COVID-19 Vaccine Hesitancy Among Canadians
metadata.journal: Canadian Journal of Behavioural Science / Revue canadienne des sciences du comportement

abstract:
The objectives of the present study were threefold: (a) examine the prevalence of COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy and rejection; (b) examine demographic, health-, and employment-related correlates of vaccine hesitancy; and (c) examine qualitative drivers of vaccine hesitancy among the Canadian population. Online, cross-sectional surveys were administered to two cohorts of Canadians between December 21, 2020, and March 30, 2021 (T1 n = 5,920, 54.1% female), and September 7, 2021, and December 7, 2021 (T2 n = 7,354, 55.6% female). Mixed methods were used within each cohort: Chi-square analyses investigated correlates of vaccine attitudes, and latent Dirichlet allocation analysis was used to deduce qualitative themes regarding drivers of vaccine hesitancy. At T2, 11% of the sample remained unvaccinated and 34% reported hesitancy towards receiving a booster dose. Correlates of vaccine hesitancy included being younger-middle-aged, having lower educational attainment, living in less densely populated areas, not being or providing care to someone who is medically vulnerable, employment insecurity, and being a non-health-care essential worker. There were significant interactions between several demographic variables. The most common reasons for hesitancy included concerns about long-term and side effects, inadequate testing, low perceived effectiveness and necessity of vaccines, and distrust of medical research, whereas logistical barriers delayed uptake among those who wanted the vaccine. Results illuminate who vaccine uptake promotion campaigns should be targeted towards, as well as the unique drivers of hesitancy towards the COVID-19 vaccine. Vaccine promotion materials should be tailored to be most relevant to identified target subpopulations and directly address concerns.

Rates of hesitancy towards receiving the COVID-19 vaccine and booster doses are high, compromising our herd immunity against COVID-19. Moreover, as new waves of COVID-19 infections continue to occur, and/or new pandemics emerge in the future, it is critical to understand the correlates and drivers of vaccine hesitancy, and how hesitancy may be most effectively addressed. Our study shows that those who are younger and middle-aged, have lower educational attainment, and live in less densely populated areas have higher rates of vaccine hesitancy. Our results also show that Canadians are hesitant to get the COVID-19 vaccine due to concerns about the long-term effects and side effects of the vaccine, thoughts that the vaccine was not adequately tested or that it is unnecessary, and mistrust of medical research and companies.

sections.paper_id: Legg et al. - 2024 - Prevalence, correlates, and drivers of COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy among Canadians.
paragraphs: COVID-19 has an infection-to-fatality ratio of 0.1%-5.5% (John Hopkins University and Medicine, 2022) and has caused over 51,500 deaths in Canada (World Health Organization [WHO], 2021b). Even with mild to moderate symptoms, a COVID-19 infection can lead to long-term complications such as chronic fatigue, muscle weakness, and cognitive difficulties (del Rio et al., 2020). Given these significant consequences, and that public health policies aimed at limiting the spread of COVID-19 (e.g., stayat-home orders, social gathering restrictions, nonessential business closures) are unsustainable (Beland et al., 2020;Varma et al., 2021), Canada has identified vaccinating all eligible Canadians against COVID-19 as a key public health priority (Public Health Agency of Canada [PHAC], 2021a). To reduce the burden on the health care system and minimize the risk of having to reintroduce public health restrictions, we need to achieve and maintain widespread immunity to the virus (Tuite et al., 2020). As of February 26, 2023, 80.7% of the total Canadian population had received a COVID-19 vaccine; however, only 22.3% had received an initial vaccine or booster dose since September 2022 (PHAC, 2021c). Given that the efficacy of the COVID-19 vaccines significantly wanes after 4-6 months, well over majority of the population is now vulnerable to COVID-19 (re)infection, escalating the risk for novel strains of COVID-19 to evade immunity (Canada House of Commons, 2021;WHO, 2021a). Vaccine hesitancy (i.e., a delay in acceptance or refusal of vaccines despite the availability of vaccination services; MacDonald & Hesitancy, 2015) is a known barrier to vaccine uptake, whether for COVID-19 or other illnesses (Buchan & Kwong, 2016;Sulis et al., 2022). Rates of COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy appear to be increasing across the globe since the start of the pandemic (de Albuquerque Veloso Machado et al., 2021). Given that there will likely be another, comparable pandemic in our lifetime that requires rapidly developed vaccines and mass public vaccination (Marani et al., 2021), understanding reasons why people may delay or refuse COVID-19 vaccination is a critical avenue to preparing for future pandemics. Indeed, this pandemic necessitated the largest population-wide vaccine campaign to combat a circulating disease in recent decades, which was situated within a highly politicized context; understanding people's reasons against becoming vaccinated against COVID-19 can inform future mass vaccination efforts.

Global vaccine hesitancy research, which has been conducted mainly in Westernized countries, indicates that certain demographic groups have higher rates of COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy. Specifically, younger age, female sex, being a racialized minority or immigrant living in majority White countries, having a religious affiliation, having lower educational attainment, and living in a rural area are positively associated with vaccine hesitancy (Frank & Arim, 2020;Gerretsen et al., 2021;Malik et al., 2020;Razai et al., 2021). Canadian research suggests that COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy is higher among those who identify as female, non-White and/or Indigenous, and non-health-care essential workers, as well as among those who have less education or believe they already had COVID-19 (Frank & Arim, 2020;Ogilvie et al., 2021). Canadian research from earlier in the pandemic also suggested that vaccine hesitancy was higher among those who are younger to middle-aged (approximately 30-60 years old; Frank & Arim, 2020;Ogilvie et al., 2021), whereas newer research suggests that there are no differences across age groups in terms of vaccine intent but less vaccine uptake among those aged 18-49 relative to those 60 or older (PHAC, 2022).

Drivers of COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy include worries that speed of vaccine development was prioritized over studying the safety, efficacy, and long-term side effects of the vaccines (Latkin et al., 2021), as well as low perceived risk or seriousness associated with contracting COVID-19 (Gerretsen et al., 2021). Research conducted in the United States suggests that 7.8% of individuals believe that COVID-19 is no more serious than the seasonal flu, and 16.7% believe that dangers of COVID-19 have been exaggerated (Latkin et al., 2022). Emerging Canadian research suggests that even with widespread availability of vaccines, suspicion about political or economic forces driving vaccine development or the pandemic, lack of knowledge about the vaccine, confusing or antivaccine messages from leaders, and concerns about pharmaceutical companies drive vaccine hesitancy (Griffith et al., 2021). Extending this research to better understand Canadians' specific reasons for vaccine hesitancy will help inform public messaging strategies and policies to increase vaccine uptake both now, and in the future, should a comparable pandemic context occur.

At this juncture, more research is needed to replicate and extend global findings within Canadians by identifying a broader range of correlates of vaccine hesitancy and specific drivers of such hesitancy. The aims of this study were threefold: (a) describe the prevalence of various vaccine attitudes and beliefs at two time points during the pandemic to help establish the reliability of our results (Spring 2021 and Fall/Winter 2021); (b) describe intersecting demographic, health-, and employment-related characteristics associated with vaccine hesitancy; and (c) describe Canadians' reasons for vaccine hesitancy and rejection. Based on previous global and Canadian research (Frank & Arim, 2020;Gerretsen et al., 2021;Malik et al., 2020;Ogilvie et al., 2021;PHAC, 2022;Razai et al., 2021), we hypothesized that vaccine hesitancy would be Board Victoria (No. 20-0320) and with the 1964 Helsinki Declaration and its later amendments or comparable ethical standards. This article does not contain any procedures involving animals performed by any of the authors. Informed consent was obtained from all individual participants included in the study.

Raw data that were used to conduct the analyses in this article will be uploaded to the University of Victoria's data repository as soon as variable recoding is complete (Borealis, the Canadian Dataverse Repository). Data cleaning procedures were preregistered and can be found on the Open Science Framework (see details in the article). Analyses were conducted in IMB SPSS Version 26 and Python's Machine Learning for Language Toolkit. No hypotheses were made pertaining to specific interaction effects due to the paucity of studies examining these interactions. We also hypothesized that safety concerns would emerge as a primary reason for COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy (Latkin et al., 2021).

sections.paper_id: Legg et al. - 2024 - Prevalence, correlates, and drivers of COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy among Canadians.
paragraphs: Two nationwide, cross-sectional, online surveys were administered to Canadians between December 21, 2020, and March 30, 2021 (T1: n = 6,472, 54.1% female), and between September 7, 2021, and December 7, 2021 (T2: n = 11,792, 55.6% female) in the context of a larger, three-wave international study assessing the impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic on mental health functioning (Rathod et al., 2020). Data from the present study were drawn from Wave 2 (T1) and Wave 3 (T2) of the larger study. Although participants were allowed to participate in more than one wave of data collection, we did not track or link participants across waves to preserve anonymity of respondents. As such, the present study presents results from two cross-sectional studies. Participants were required to be: (a) living in Canada at the time of the survey and (b) aged 17 years or older. Participants were recruited from a Canadian survey and data services company (Maru/Blue) using quota-based sampling to match the sex, age, and province/territory of residence composition of the 2019 Canadian census. These responses were supplemented with participants recruited via social media posts and community advertisements, which aimed to increase responses from those aged 18-24 and 65+. Social media recruitment was conducted without demographic quotas. As such, the survey design is a mix of convenience and nonrandom stratified sampling.

sections.paper_id: Legg et al. - 2024 - Prevalence, correlates, and drivers of COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy among Canadians.
paragraphs: Study advertisements contained a link directing participants to the online consent form and survey hosted on the Qualtrics Survey platform. Consenting participants were screened for eligibility and then began the surveys. The T1 and T2 surveys were composed of predominantly close-ended, nonrandomized questions, and each took approximately 15-20 min to complete (with some variability due to skip logic [i.e., participants only completed items relevant to them]). The survey items used in the present study queried participant demographics, experiences during the pandemic, COVID-19 vaccine uptake, and attitudes towards COVID-19 vaccines. Participants recruited via social media were entered into a draw for various gift cards, and those recruited via Maru/Blue received points in accordance with Maru's compensation structure. All aspects of the study were approved by our institution's Human Research Ethics Board.

sections.paper_id: Legg et al. - 2024 - Prevalence, correlates, and drivers of COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy among Canadians.
paragraphs: At T1, all participants were asked about their intention to get a COVID-19 vaccine. Those who reported that they were vaccine hesitant or vaccine rejecting, respectively, were asked in openended format to describe "… your main reasons for [being undecided about getting the vaccine] or [for deciding not to get the vaccine]." At T2, participants were asked whether they had received one or more doses of a COVID-19 vaccine. Those who did not endorse receiving one or more doses of a COVID-19 vaccine (i.e., who said they had not received a vaccine but planned to [delayed vaccination], were undecided about receiving a vaccine, or would not receive the vaccine), were asked to select their reasons for not yet receiving a vaccine from a list of common reasons that was created based on our T1 results.

sections.header_norm: demographic employment and health history variables
paragraphs: Participants reported their self-identified gender (i.e., male, female, or nonbinary/transgender/other), age group, highest level of attained education, race, religion, province or territory of residence, and population density where they lived (i.e., metropolis, large city, small city, or rural area). For health-related variables, participants were asked if they considered themselves medically vulnerable to COVID-19, provided daily care to someone who is vulnerable to COVID-19, and if they, or anyone they knew, had been diagnosed with or tested positive for COVID-19. Finally, regarding employment, participants were asked if they had lost or were worried about losing their job due to the pandemic, were essential workers, or worked in an environment that required contact with people who may display symptoms of COVID-19.

sections.paper_id: Legg et al. - 2024 - Prevalence, correlates, and drivers of COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy among Canadians.
paragraphs: A total of 6,472 participants responded to the T1 survey, and 11,792 participants responded to the T2 survey. Once each enrolment quota had been met, participants in that quota group were automatically redirected to the end of the survey. Responses were also screened for ineligibility, failed attention-check questions, improbably fast completion, and high levels of itemlevel missingness (>90% of survey items). The final samples, following quota-based and quality-check removals, were 5,920 people (Maru/Blue: n = 5,229, social media: n = 691) at T1 and 7,354 people (Maru/Blue: n = 5,985, social media: n = 1,369) at T2. Data cleaning procedures were preregistered and can be found on the Open Science Framework (https://osf.io/4d6tq/?vie w_only=fbd188d87f7a45d7b9e53f7484bf1cf8).foot_0

sections.paper_id: Legg et al. - 2024 - Prevalence, correlates, and drivers of COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy among Canadians.
paragraphs: All quantitative analyses were conducted in IBM SPSS software, Version 26. Aim 1 was addressed using descriptive statistics summarizing endorsement of vaccine attitudes and beliefs. Aim 2 was addressed using chi-square analyses to compare rates of hesitancy or rejection across demographic, health-, and employment-related variables. Stratified chi-square analyses were used to examine whether several key demographic variables (which were dichotomized to facilitate interpretation of stratified analyses), including gender (male/female), education (fewer than 4/4+ years postsecondary), or race (White/non-White), moderated the focal effects of other demographic variables (i.e., age, population density, education). Participants aged 17-24 were excluded from stratified chi-square analyses of education as those under 25 have not had the chance to attain higher levels of education.

Aim 3 was addressed using a mixture of qualitative and quantitative methods. Latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA; Blei et al., 2003), a natural language processing technique, was used at T1 to identify qualitative themes in the open-ended survey responses and to examine theme prevalence. LDA identifies latent topics in text data based on patterns and frequencies of word co-occurrences using Python's Machine Learning for Language Toolkit. Prior to model fitting, open-ended responses provided in French were translated into English by a bilingual research assistant. Translations were then checked by a second bilingual research assistant, and minor translation discrepancies were resolved by consensus of the two research assistants. Next, stop-words (e.g., "the," "it," "there") were removed, and all remaining words were lemmatized. To fit the LDA, we compared coherence scores for models with 2, 4, 6, 8, or 10 latent topics, with the aim of balancing model coherence and parsimony. Latent topics were interpreted by two graduate-level raters (coauthors NKL and ACS) based on the 10 most frequently appearing words in each topic and by examining discrete pieces of text that were identified as reflecting this dominant topic. Final topic labels were decided by consensus of the two raters. At T2, descriptive statistics were used to summarize the prevalence of various reasons for vaccine hesitancy, as this question was asked in a multiple-choice format wherein the response options were informed by T1 responses.

sections.paper_id: Legg et al. - 2024 - Prevalence, correlates, and drivers of COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy among Canadians.
paragraphs: Of the 5,317 participants who reported their vaccination plans and attitudes at T1, 48.4% planned to get a vaccine as soon as it was available, 24.9% were willing to be vaccinated but wanted to see how rollout proceeded, 14.9% were vaccine hesitant (i.e., "I'm not sure/I don't know"), 9.8% were vaccine refusing (i.e., "No, I do not want to get the vaccine"), and 2.0% could not be vaccinated for medical reasons. Supplemental questions regarding specific vaccine attitudes are shown in Supplemental Table B. A total of 24.6% of respondents "somewhat" or "strongly" believed that vaccines had not been adequately tested for side effects, and 12.7% "somewhat" or "strongly" believed that vaccines would be ineffective in preventing COVID-19 infection.

Of the 7,354 participants who reported their vaccination status at T2, 81.9% had received two of two vaccine doses, 6.9% had received one of two doses, and 0.5% had received one of one dose of the COVID-19 vaccine. The remaining 10.7% of the sample were unvaccinated (i.e., had received 0 COVID-19 vaccine dose). Of these unvaccinated participants, 36.6% planned to get vaccinated, 21.6% were hesitant (i.e., "I haven't decided"), 35.6% were vaccine refusing (i.e., "No, I will not get the vaccine"), and 6.3% could not be vaccinated for medical reasons. Of the 7,155 T2 participants who responded to a question about their intention to receive a booster dose, 63.2% endorsed being very likely to receive a booster, 21.5% endorsed hesitancy (i.e., "somewhat likely"), and 12.6% rejected further doses (i.e., "not at all likely").

sections.container_raw: Aim 2: Characteristics Associated With Vaccine Hesitancy or Rejection
sections.container_norm: aim 2 characteristics associated with vaccine hesitancy or rejection
paragraphs: Tables 1 and 2 present demographic differences in vaccine attitudes at T1 and T2. At both T1 and T2, older adults (i.e., those aged 65+) had the lowest proportion of vaccine hesitancy relative to all other age groups, and older adults and very young adults (aged 17-20 years old) had significantly lower rates of vaccine rejection relative to young-middle-aged adults. Respondents with lower educational attainment endorsed greater vaccine hesitancy and rejection relative to those who completed at least some postsecondary education. Respondents living in Quebec endorsed greater vaccine hesitancy relative to those living in British Columbia, and similarly, people living in Alberta endorsed greater vaccine rejection relative to those living in British Columbia. Rural respondents endorsed greater vaccine hesitancy relative to those living in more populous areas, and rural and small city respondents endorsed greater vaccine rejection relative to those living in more populous areas.

Some results pertaining to demographic correlates were inconsistent across surveys. Females endorsed greater vaccine hesitancy relative to males at T1, whereas the opposite was found at T2. People who self-identified as multiracial or as a racialized minority endorsed greater vaccine hesitancy relative to White-identified people at T1, and White and multiracial people endorsed greater vaccine rejection than racialized minorities at T1, but race was not associated with vaccine attitudes at T2. At T1, religious affiliation was not associated with vaccine attitudes, but at T2, people who had a religious affiliation endorsed greater vaccine hesitancy and rejection than those who did not have an affiliation.

paragraphs: Table 3 summarizes vaccine attitudes by health-related indicators. There were no significant differences in vaccine attitudes based on COVID-19 exposure in the T1 or T2 surveys. Respondents who provided care to someone who was medically vulnerable to COVID-19 endorsed lower vaccine hesitancy and rejection relative to those who did not provide such care or who were unsure if they provided such care. Respondents who did not consider themselves to be medically vulnerable to COVID-19 endorsed greater rates of vaccine rejection relative to those who did consider themselves medically vulnerable.

sections.paper_id: Legg et al. - 2024 - Prevalence, correlates, and drivers of COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy among Canadians.
Legg et al. - 2024 - Prevalence, correlates, and drivers of COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy among Canadians.
paragraphs: Table 4 summarizes vaccine attitudes by employment characteristics. Notably, in October 2021 (i.e., in the middle of T2 data collection), vaccination against COVID-19 became mandated for most federal public servants. As such, T2 results should be interpreted with caution.

At T1, those who experienced job loss during the pandemic endorsed greater vaccine rejection (but not hesitancy) than those who did not lose their jobs. At T2, those reporting job loss endorsed greater hesitancy (but not rejection) than those who did not lose their jobs. At T1, those who were worried about future job loss endorsed greater vaccine hesitancy (but not rejection) than those who were not worried about job loss. At T2, those who were worried about future job loss endorsed greater vaccine hesitancy and rejection than those who were not worried about job loss. At T1, those who had work-related contact with people displaying COVID-19 symptoms reported greater vaccine hesitancy relative to those who did not; however, this result was not replicated at T2. At T1, those who self-identified as a non-health-care essential worker endorsed greater vaccine rejection relative to those who did not identify this as their job profession. At T2, those identifying as a non-health-care essential worker endorsed greater hesitancy and rejection than those who did not endorse this as their professional role.

sections.paper_id: Legg et al. - 2024 - Prevalence, correlates, and drivers of COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy among Canadians.
paragraphs: At T2, participants who had not yet received a vaccine but planned to (n = 344; 4.6% of the full T2 sample) reported delaying their vaccination because (participants were asked to select all reasons that apply): nearby vaccine centres were booked up (38.4%), they wanted to see how others reacted to the vaccine first (30.2%), it was not a priority (22.1%), they were unable to get time off work (9%), it required lengthy/difficult travel to get to a vaccine centre (8.1%), and they were unable to get childcare (3.5%). Participants who were undecided about vaccination (n = 203; 2.8% of the T2 sample) reported concern about the long-term effects and side effects from the vaccine (61.6%), mistrust of the government/pharmaceutical companies (57.6%), concern about the lack of information about the vaccine (46.3%), uncertainty regarding vaccine efficacy (33.5%), lack of concern about contracting COVID-19 (18.7%), and medical concerns related to the vaccine (10.3%). Reasons pertaining to religious concerns, having already contracted COVID-19, and belief that COVID-19 is a hoax were endorsed with similar infrequency (1%-5%). Participants who did not intend to become vaccinated (n = 335; 4.6% of the T2 sample) reported concerns about the long-term and side effects of the vaccine (74.0%), mistrust of the government/ pharmaceutical companies (66.3%), lack of information about the vaccine (55.8%), uncertainty regarding vaccine efficacy (50.7%), being unconcerned about contracting COVID-19 (43.9%), medical concerns around the vaccine (24.4%), and that they did not receive vaccines in general (18.2%). Reasons pertaining to religious reasons, thinking COVID-19 is a hoax, or having already contracted COVID-19, were endorsed with similar infrequency (9%-14%).

sections.paper_id: Legg et al. - 2024 - Prevalence, correlates, and drivers of COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy among Canadians.
paragraphs: This study expands what was previously known about COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy in three ways: First, we examine intersecting demographic characteristics, as well as pandemic-specific experiences (e.g., job loss, caregiving) that are associated with vaccine hesitancy and rejection; second, we test these correlates at two different time points, in separate samples, to help establish the reliability of our results; and third, we used a mixed-method approach to understand reasons for delaying or refusing vaccination.

Major findings are threefold. First, in Fall 2021, when COVID-19 vaccines were widely and freely available in Canada, 11% of our survey respondents remained unvaccinated (consistent with current reported population vaccination rates; PHAC, 2021c); and 34% reported at least some hesitancy to receive additional booster doses. These results, along with the ongoing emergence of new COVID-19 virus strains, indicate that the need to monitor and address vaccine hesitancy in the context of the COVID-19 pandemic Note. -Denote cells not reported as cell size is less than 10 people; T = time. Numerical values represent the percentage of endorsement for each vaccine attitude within each level of a given variable (i.e., within each row). Subscripts denote post hoc test results in comparing percentage of endorsement between each level of a given variable, within each vaccine attitude (i.e., differences within columns). Different subscripts denote a significant difference between levels of a given variable within a given vaccine attitude (i.e., significant difference between column cells), whereas matching subscripts denote no significant difference between column cells.

is far from over. Second, some demographic characteristics appear to be more reliably associated with vaccine hesitancy than others. Consistent with our hypothesis and previous research (Frank & Arim, 2020;Malik et al., 2020;Ogilvie et al., 2021), having lower educational attainment and living in less densely populated areas were associated with higher vaccine hesitancy at both time points, along with being younger to middle-aged (i.e., between 21 and 64; no hypothesis was made for age). However, inconsistent with our hypotheses, sex and race were not reliably associated with vaccine attitudes, which may be explained by interactions between demographic characteristics. For example, our moderation results indicated that higher education was associated with more hesitancy among non-White people, those living in more populous areas, and those aged 35-44, but was a protective factor for other age groups, White people, or those living in less populous places. We further expand knowledge by exploring employment and health-related correlates of COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy. Features such as COVID-19-related job loss or job insecurity, being a non-health-care essential worker, not being medically vulnerable to COVID-19, and not providing care for someone who is medically vulnerable, were associated with increased vaccine hesitancy. Notably, these are characteristics of younger adults and may help explain why young-middle-aged adults were more reluctant to be vaccinated than their older counterparts. Finally, this study characterizes reasons for vaccine hesitancy among Canadians after COVID-19 vaccines became widely available. Consistent with our hypothesis, concerns about long-term effects and side effects, as well as perceptions of inadequate testing of vaccines, were the most prevalent reasons for hesitancy. Our results also indicate that questions about vaccine effectiveness and necessity, along with distrust of medical research, pharmaceutical companies, or government, were additional concerns driving vaccine hesitancy.

Unfortunately, but perhaps not surprisingly, our results indicate that vaccine hesitancy remains an important concern even after COVID-19 vaccines became widely and freely available to Canadians. Consistent with our results indicating more hesitancy towards receiving a booster dose versus an initial vaccine dose, vaccination rates have slowed considerably in Canada (PHAC, 2021c). Despite policies and campaigns to increase vaccine uptake (PHAC, 2021b), serious waves of COVID-19 infections necessitating ongoing, or reimplementation of, public health restrictions are being documented even in countries and areas with high vaccination rates (Government of Canada, 2022;Zhu et al., 2022). This highlights the continued vulnerability of our population and health care system to COVID-19 and reaffirms the importance of informing and increasing the efficacy of efforts to increase vaccination and booster dose rates. Moreover, research indicates there will be additional pandemics in our lifetime that are comparable to the COVID-19 pandemic (Marani et al., 2021), which may necessitate the rapid development of new vaccines and mass vaccination. Given that the WHO named vaccine hesitancy as one of the top ten threats to global health (WHO, 2019), understanding the correlates and drivers of vaccine hesitancy during the COVID-19 pandemic is a unique and critical avenue to illuminating how to best address hesitancy in the future.

A key question is where and to whom interventions that aim to increase vaccine confidence and uptake should be directed. Consistent with past research (e.g., Frank & Arim, 2020;Malik et al., 2020;Ogilvie et al., 2021;Oliveira et al., 2021), our results suggest that in general, maximal impact might be achieved if interventions are targeted towards younger-middle-aged adults, those with lower educational attainment, and those living in rural or less populous areas, as these variables appear to be reliably associated with greater vaccine hesitancy and/or rejection. Our results are in concordance with past findings suggesting that vaccine hesitancy is lower at more extreme ends of the adult age span (i.e., 17-20 and 65+), and that adults towards the middle of the adult age span would benefit from hesitancy interventions (Frank & Arim, 2020;Ogilvie et al., 2021). However, other Canadian research suggests that COVID-19 vaccine uptake is lower among those aged 18-49 relative to those 60 or older (PHAC, 2022). It may be that although very young adults endorse accepting attitudes towards vaccination, they have a lower risk perception of contracting COVID-19 and therefore, are slower at taking steps to Note. -Denote cells not reported, as cell size is less than 10 people; T = time. sections.text:
This study expands what was previously known about COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy in three ways: First, we examine intersecting demographic characteristics, as well as pandemic-specific experiences (e.g., job loss, caregiving) that are associated with vaccine hesitancy and rejection; second, we test these correlates at two different time points, in separate samples, to help establish the reliability of our results; and third, we used a mixed-method approach to understand reasons for delaying or refusing vaccination.

Major findings are threefold. First, in Fall 2021, when COVID-19 vaccines were widely and freely available in Canada, 11% of our survey respondents remained unvaccinated (consistent with current reported population vaccination rates; PHAC, 2021c); and 34% reported at least some hesitancy to receive additional booster doses. These results, along with the ongoing emergence of new COVID-19 virus strains, indicate that the need to monitor and address vaccine hesitancy in the context of the COVID-19 pandemic Note. -Denote cells not reported as cell size is less than 10 people; T = time. Numerical values represent the percentage of endorsement for each vaccine attitude within each level of a given variable (i.e., within each row). Subscripts denote post hoc test results in comparing percentage of endorsement between each level of a given variable, within each vaccine attitude (i.e., differences within columns). Different subscripts denote a significant difference between levels of a given variable within a given vaccine attitude (i.e., significant difference between column cells), whereas matching subscripts denote no significant difference between column cells.

is far from over. Second, some demographic characteristics appear to be more reliably associated with vaccine hesitancy than others. Consistent with our hypothesis and previous research (Frank & Arim, 2020;Malik et al., 2020;Ogilvie et al., 2021), having lower educational attainment and living in less densely populated areas were associated with higher vaccine hesitancy at both time points, along with being younger to middle-aged (i.e., between 21 and 64; no hypothesis was made for age). However, inconsistent with our hypotheses, sex and race were not reliably associated with vaccine attitudes, which may be explained by interactions between demographic characteristics. For example, our moderation results indicated that higher education was associated with more hesitancy among non-White people, those living in more populous areas, and those aged 35-44, but was a protective factor for other age groups, White people, or those living in less populous places. We further expand knowledge by exploring employment and health-related correlates of COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy. Features such as COVID-19-related job loss or job insecurity, being a non-health-care essential worker, not being medically vulnerable to COVID-19, and not providing care for someone who is medically vulnerable, were associated with increased vaccine hesitancy. Notably, these are characteristics of younger adults and may help explain why young-middle-aged adults were more reluctant to be vaccinated than their older counterparts. Finally, this study characterizes reasons for vaccine hesitancy among Canadians after COVID-19 vaccines became widely available. Consistent with our hypothesis, concerns about long-term effects and side effects, as well as perceptions of inadequate testing of vaccines, were the most prevalent reasons for hesitancy. Our results also indicate that questions about vaccine effectiveness and necessity, along with distrust of medical research, pharmaceutical companies, or government, were additional concerns driving vaccine hesitancy.

Unfortunately, but perhaps not surprisingly, our results indicate that vaccine hesitancy remains an important concern even after COVID-19 vaccines became widely and freely available to Canadians. Consistent with our results indicating more hesitancy towards receiving a booster dose versus an initial vaccine dose, vaccination rates have slowed considerably in Canada (PHAC, 2021c). Despite policies and campaigns to increase vaccine uptake (PHAC, 2021b), serious waves of COVID-19 infections necessitating ongoing, or reimplementation of, public health restrictions are being documented even in countries and areas with high vaccination rates (Government of Canada, 2022;Zhu et al., 2022). This highlights the continued vulnerability of our population and health care system to COVID-19 and reaffirms the importance of informing and increasing the efficacy of efforts to increase vaccination and booster dose rates. Moreover, research indicates there will be additional pandemics in our lifetime that are comparable to the COVID-19 pandemic (Marani et al., 2021), which may necessitate the rapid development of new vaccines and mass vaccination. Given that the WHO named vaccine hesitancy as one of the top ten threats to global health (WHO, 2019), understanding the correlates and drivers of vaccine hesitancy during the COVID-19 pandemic is a unique and critical avenue to illuminating how to best address hesitancy in the future.

A key question is where and to whom interventions that aim to increase vaccine confidence and uptake should be directed. Consistent with past research (e.g., Frank & Arim, 2020;Malik et al., 2020;Ogilvie et al., 2021;Oliveira et al., 2021), our results suggest that in general, maximal impact might be achieved if interventions are targeted towards younger-middle-aged adults, those with lower educational attainment, and those living in rural or less populous areas, as these variables appear to be reliably associated with greater vaccine hesitancy and/or rejection. Our results are in concordance with past findings suggesting that vaccine hesitancy is lower at more extreme ends of the adult age span (i.e., 17-20 and 65+), and that adults towards the middle of the adult age span would benefit from hesitancy interventions (Frank & Arim, 2020;Ogilvie et al., 2021). However, other Canadian research suggests that COVID-19 vaccine uptake is lower among those aged 18-49 relative to those 60 or older (PHAC, 2022). It may be that although very young adults endorse accepting attitudes towards vaccination, they have a lower risk perception of contracting COVID-19 and therefore, are slower at taking steps to Note. -Denote cells not reported, as cell size is less than 10 people; T = time. Numerical values represent the percentage of endorsement for each vaccine attitude within each column, across levels of the independent variables. Subscripts denote post hoc test results in comparing the percentage of endorsement between levels of a given moderating variable (i.e., differences between columns). Different subscripts denote a significant difference between levels of a given moderating variable within a given vaccine attitude (i.e., significant difference between column cells), whereas matching subscripts denote no significant difference between column cells.

COVID-19 VACCINE HESITANCY AMONG CANADIANS receive a vaccination relative to older adults. Indeed, our results, along with other studies, indicate that not being medically vulnerable to COVID-19 and perceiving vaccination as low priority are associated with greater vaccine hesitancy (Kim et al., 2022;Soares et al., 2021). Accordingly, those aged 21-64 may be less worried about the physical effects of contracting COVID-19 or may be exposed to vaccination misinformation via social media more often than older adults, rendering them more hesitant towards vaccination (Kim et al., 2022). Interventions aimed at increasing vaccine uptake may be most successful if directed towards those who are hesitant rather than rejecting of the vaccine (such as those who do not consider themselves medically vulnerable), as hesitant folks may be more amenable to vaccine uptake intervention efforts. However, many of our variables were correlated with both hesitant and rejecting attitudes. Our results may also apply to future situations where mass vaccination uptake is required; younger and middle-aged adults who do not perceive themselves as medically vulnerable may need to be provided with tailored, reliable information about the health impacts of illness, despite their age or medical status. Interestingly, gender, race, and religion appeared to be less reliably associated with vaccine attitudes. Specifically, greater vaccine hesitancy was associated with being female sex and non-White at T1 and with being male sex and White or multiracial at T2. Moreover, we did not find an association between religious affiliation and vaccine hesitancy at T1, but at T2, and consistent with past research, religious affiliation was associated with greater vaccine hesitancy and rejection (Oliveira et al., 2021;Williams et al., 2021). One explanation for these inconsistent results, particularly for gender and race, may be related to the role of intersecting identities. Many of our results pertaining to demographic correlates were moderated by other demographic variables. For example, among those living in densely populated areas, vaccine hesitancy was higher among those who were highly educated, non-White, and/or male, whereas in less populous areas, vaccine hesitancy was greatest among those with less education, who were White, and/or were female. Given that more education has been consistently associated with lower vaccine hesitancy (Frank & Arim, 2020;Gerretsen et al., 2021;Malik et al., 2020;Razai et al., 2021), our moderation results highlight the need for additional research to examine the mechanisms that underpin these interaction effects and vaccine-acceptance decisions. Moreover, although research indicates that there are general correlates of vaccine hesitancy, our results indicate that there are important caveats and nuances to these findings: our results illuminate the specific demographic subgroups with greater vaccine hesitancy and highlight the importance of considering intersecting identities when creating targeted vaccine uptake campaigns.

There may be explanations for our mixed results across T1 and T2 beyond interaction effects. For gender, results from our most recent data suggest that males endorse more vaccine hesitancy than females. This is in line with current Canadian statistics indicating that women currently have slightly higher vaccination rates than men across ages (PHAC, 2021c). Given that providing direct care for someone who is vulnerable towards COVID-19 was associated with greater vaccine acceptance in our data and that caregiving duties disproportionately fall to women (Moyser & Burlock, 2018), it may be that caregiving partially explains higher vaccine acceptance among women. Contrary to research suggesting vaccine hesitancy in women is driven by concerns about fertility and pregnancy (Stojanovic et al., 2021), we did not find consistent interactions between gender and age nor observe concertation of vaccine hesitancy among women of childbearing years. In terms of religion, it may be that as the pandemic progressed, some religious groups and leaders formed or solidified a position that did not encourage the uptake of COVID-19 vaccination, resulting in mixed findings around religious affiliation and vaccine attitudes at different time points in the pandemic (Kibongani Volet et al., 2022). Our results highlight the need to further explore the nuances and mechanisms that exist within these inconsistent findings.

Previous research suggests that effective interventions should be tailored to address specific reasons for vaccine hesitancy (Jarrett et al., 2015), highlighting the importance of identifying drivers of COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy among Canadians. Though a large body of research documents drivers of hesitancy for influenza vaccines (e.g., inconvenience, needle phobia, incorrect beliefs about vaccine efficacy, fear of serious side effects; Ryan et al., 2019), it is unclear if these results generalize to COVID-19 vaccines given the unique conditions under which these vaccines were created. Consistent with earlier research investigating drivers of COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy (Latkin et al., 2022), our results suggest that Canadians who endorse hesitancy have concerns about the safety of the vaccine, including its short-and long-term effects, as well as queries around adequacy of testing. These vaccine safety concerns are likely related to the expedited speed at which the vaccine was created and authorized for administration (Brown et al., 2022). COVID-19 VACCINE HESITANCY AMONG CANADIANS receive a vaccination relative to older adults. Indeed, our results, along with other studies, indicate that not being medically vulnerable to COVID-19 and perceiving vaccination as low priority are associated with greater vaccine hesitancy (Kim et al., 2022;Soares et al., 2021). Accordingly, those aged 21-64 may be less worried about the physical effects of contracting COVID-19 or may be exposed to vaccination misinformation via social media more often than older adults, rendering them more hesitant towards vaccination (Kim et al., 2022). Interventions aimed at increasing vaccine uptake may be most successful if directed towards those who are hesitant rather than rejecting of the vaccine (such as those who do not consider themselves medically vulnerable), as hesitant folks may be more amenable to vaccine uptake intervention efforts. However, many of our variables were correlated with both hesitant and rejecting attitudes. Our results may also apply to future situations where mass vaccination uptake is required; younger and middle-aged adults who do not perceive themselves as medically vulnerable may need to be provided with tailored, reliable information about the health impacts of illness, despite their age or medical status. Interestingly, gender, race, and religion appeared to be less reliably associated with vaccine attitudes. Specifically, greater vaccine hesitancy was associated with being female sex and non-White at T1 and with being male sex and White or multiracial at T2. Moreover, we did not find an association between religious affiliation and vaccine hesitancy at T1, but at T2, and consistent with past research, religious affiliation was associated with greater vaccine hesitancy and rejection (Oliveira et al., 2021;Williams et al., 2021). One explanation for these inconsistent results, particularly for gender and race, may be related to the role of intersecting identities. Many of our results pertaining to demographic correlates were moderated by other demographic variables. For example, among those living in densely populated areas, vaccine hesitancy was higher among those who were highly educated, non-White, and/or male, whereas in less populous areas, vaccine hesitancy was greatest among those with less education, who were White, and/or were female. Given that more education has been consistently associated with lower vaccine hesitancy (Frank & Arim, 2020;Gerretsen et al., 2021;Malik et al., 2020;Razai et al., 2021), our moderation results highlight the need for additional research to examine the mechanisms that underpin these interaction effects and vaccine-acceptance decisions. Moreover, although research indicates that there are general correlates of vaccine hesitancy, our results indicate that there are important caveats and nuances to these findings: our results illuminate the specific demographic subgroups with greater vaccine hesitancy and highlight the importance of considering intersecting identities when creating targeted vaccine uptake campaigns.

There may be explanations for our mixed results across T1 and T2 beyond interaction effects. For gender, results from our most recent data suggest that males endorse more vaccine hesitancy than females. This is in line with current Canadian statistics indicating that women currently have slightly higher vaccination rates than men across ages (PHAC, 2021c). Given that providing direct care for someone who is vulnerable towards COVID-19 was associated with greater vaccine acceptance in our data and that caregiving duties disproportionately fall to women (Moyser & Burlock, 2018), it may be that caregiving partially explains higher vaccine acceptance among women. Contrary to research suggesting vaccine hesitancy in women is driven by concerns about fertility and pregnancy (Stojanovic et al., 2021), we did not find consistent interactions between gender and age nor observe concertation of vaccine hesitancy among women of childbearing years. In terms of religion, it may be that as the pandemic progressed, some religious groups and leaders formed or solidified a position that did not encourage the uptake of COVID-19 vaccination, resulting in mixed findings around religious affiliation and vaccine attitudes at different time points in the pandemic (Kibongani Volet et al., 2022). Our results highlight the need to further explore the nuances and mechanisms that exist within these inconsistent findings.

Previous research suggests that effective interventions should be tailored to address specific reasons for vaccine hesitancy (Jarrett et al., 2015), highlighting the importance of identifying drivers of COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy among Canadians. Though a large body of research documents drivers of hesitancy for influenza vaccines (e.g., inconvenience, needle phobia, incorrect beliefs about vaccine efficacy, fear of serious side effects; Ryan et al., 2019), it is unclear if these results generalize to COVID-19 vaccines given the unique conditions under which these vaccines were created. These vaccine safety concerns are likely related to the expedited speed at which the vaccine was created and authorized for administration (Brown et al., 2022). Other Canadians report that they do not perceive vaccines as necessary, have questions about vaccine efficacy, and lack trust for medical and government organizations that are promoting the vaccines. sections.paper_id: Legg et al. - 2024 - Prevalence, correlates, and drivers of COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy among Canadians.
paragraphs: Our study offers several strengths, including a large, semistratified sample, and mixed-method approach; however, there are limitations that should be considered. First, respondents selfselected into participation. Our study was advertised as examining mental health and well-being during the pandemic, which may have appealed to those who are more psychologically minded or have an interest in mental health or health, which may have biased our results pertaining to vaccination attitudes. Moreover, our study was administered online, thereby excluding those who do not have access to internet. As such, results are not completely representative of vaccine attitudes within the general Canadian population.

Second, because participant age was reported using age bands (e.g., 25-34) rather than specific ages, important age-related differences within bands, particularly our oldest group (i.e., aged 65 or older), may be masked. Third, although some of our results replicated across both time points, other results did not. Although the demographic compositions of our two data sets are comparable, we cannot make direct comparisons or conclusions regarding changes or differences in vaccine attitudes between timepoints given the cross-sectional design and unique samples included in each survey.

Future research should continue studying patterns and drivers of vaccine hesitancy among Canadians. Given that vaccine rollout plans and information campaigns are managed at the provincial or territorial level in Canada, and that our results indicated some differences in vaccine attitudes between provinces, future research should examine how varying regional vaccination strategies impact vaccine uptake and attitudes. Moreover, research specific to marginalized and minority populations, especially newcomers and refugees, people who are marginally housed, and people who experience serious substance use or mental health concerns, is needed to capture vaccine concerns that may be unique to these populations. Longitudinal studies could inform how the prevalence, correlates, and reasons for vaccine attitudes may have changed within individuals as vaccination rates and restrictions have changed and different vaccine-promoting policies or interventions have been deployed. Finally, research investigating how to modify vaccine attitudes and increase vaccine uptake is crucial.
